title: Westpac audit chair sat in on pitch talks despite KPMG conflict fears
full_text:
Westpac director Peter Nash sat in on meetings when KPMG, EY and Deloitte pitched for the bank's $32 million a year audit, despite the bank's assurances that the former KPMG chairman was "not involved" in the tender.
Nash, who spent 25 years at KPMG and chaired the firm from 2011 to 2017, now chairs Westpac's board audit committee. Rivals were unhappy with his presence but stayed silent to protect bids, and insiders said Nash was "engaged in questioning" the pitch teams. The presence of Nash in bid meetings appears to contradict the bank's assurances to a parliamentary committee that he was "not involved" because of "potential or perceived conflicts of interest arising from his former role as chairman of one of the audit firms tendering".
Westpac, which declined to comment, has previously said Nash did not have a vote on the audit and was excluded from the board subcommittee formed to manage the tender. KPMG also declined to comment.
Nash, a veteran company director, also stayed at the house of KPMG chairman Martin Sheppard during the audit pitch.
On Friday, The Australian Financial Review reported Westpac is considering putting its audit work back out to tender only two years after awarding the contract to KPMG over a whistleblower scandal at the firm. It is also weighing asking KPMG to help cover the multimillion-dollar cost of that process. Companies p14
